sentences.text: This is an alternative presentation of the RVWMO memory model in operational style.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: It aims to admit exactly the same extensional behavior as the axiomatic presentation: for any given program, admitting an execution if and only if the axiomatic presentation allows it.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: The axiomatic presentation is defined as a predicate on complete candidate executions.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: In contrast, this operational presentation has an abstract microarchitectural flavor: it is expressed as a state machine, with states that are an abstract representation of hardware machine states, and with explicit out-of-order and speculative execution (but abstracting from more implementation-specific microarchitectural details such as register renaming, store buffers, cache hierarchies, cache protocols, etc.).
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: As such, it can provide useful intuition.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: It can also construct executions incrementally, making it possible to interactively and randomly explore the behavior of larger examples, while the axiomatic model requires complete candidate executions over which the axioms can be checked.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: The operational presentation covers mixed-size execution, with potentially overlapping memory accesses of different power-of-two byte sizes.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: Misaligned accesses are broken up into single-byte accesses.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: The operational model, together with a fragment of the RISC-V ISA semantics (RV64I and A), are integrated into the `rmem` exploration tool (https://github.com/rems-project/rmem). `rmem` can explore litmus tests (see ) and small ELF binaries exhaustively, pseudorandomly and interactively.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: In `rmem`, the ISA semantics is expressed explicitly in Sail (see https://github.com/rems-project/sail for the Sail language, and https://github.com/rems-project/sail-riscv for the RISC-V ISA model), and the concurrency semantics is expressed in Lem (see https://github.com/rems-project/lem for the Lem language).
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: `rmem` has a command-line interface and a web-interface.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: The web-interface runs entirely on the client side, and is provided online together with a library of litmus tests: http://www.cl.cam.ac.uk/.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: The command-line interface is faster than the web-interface, specially in exhaustive mode.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: Below is an informal introduction of the model states and transitions.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: The description of the formal model starts in the next subsection.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: Terminology: In contrast to the axiomatic presentation, here every memory operation is either a load or a store.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: Hence, AMOs give rise to two distinct memory operations, a load and a store.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: When used in conjunction with `instruction`, the terms `load` and `store` refer to instructions that give rise to such memory operations.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: As such, both include AMO instructions.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: The term `acquire` refers to an instruction (or its memory operation) with the acquire-RCpc or acquire-RCsc annotation.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: The term `release` refers to an instruction (or its memory operation) with the release-RCpc or release-RCsc annotation.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: Model states: A model state consists of a shared memory and a tuple of hart states.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model
Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: The shared memory state records all the memory store operations that have propagated so far, in the order they propagated (this can be made more efficient, but for simplicity of the presentation we keep it this way).
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: Each hart state consists principally of a tree of instruction instances, some of which have been finished, and some of which have not.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: Non-finished instruction instances can be subject to restart, e.g. if they depend on an out-of-order or speculative load that turns out to be unsound.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: Conditional branch and indirect jump instructions may have multiple successors in the instruction tree.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: When such instruction is finished, any untaken alternative paths are discarded.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: Each instruction instance in the instruction tree has a state that includes an execution state of the intra-instruction semantics (the ISA pseudocode for this instruction).
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: The model uses a formalization of the intra-instruction semantics in Sail.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: One can think of the execution state of an instruction as a representation of the pseudocode control state, pseudocode call stack, and local variable values.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: An instruction instance state also includes information about the instance's memory and register footprints, its register reads and writes, its memory operations, whether it is finished, etc.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: The model defines, for any model state, the set of allowed transitions, each of which is a single atomic step to a new abstract machine state.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: Execution of a single instruction will typically involve many transitions, and they may be interleaved in operational-model execution with transitions arising from other instructions.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: Each transition arises from a single instruction instance; it will change the state of that instance, and it may depend on or change the rest of its hart state and the shared memory state, but it does not depend on other hart states, and it will not change them.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: The transitions are introduced below and defined in , with a precondition and a construction of the post-transition model state for each.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: Transitions for all instructions:
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: : This transition represents a fetch and decode of a new instruction instance, as a program order successor of a previously fetched instruction instance (or the initial fetch address).
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: The model assumes the instruction memory is fixed; it does not describe the behavior of self-modifying code.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: In particular, the transition does not generate memory load operations, and the shared memory is not involved in the transition.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: Instead, the model depends on an external oracle that provides an opcode when given a memory location.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: [circle] * : This is a write of a register value.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: : This is a read of a register value from the most recent program-order-predecessor instruction instance that writes to that register.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: : This covers pseudocode internal computation: arithmetic, function calls, etc.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: : At this point the instruction pseudocode is done, the instruction cannot be restarted, memory accesses cannot be discarded, and all memory effects have taken place.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: For conditional branch and indirect jump instructions, any program order successors that were fetched from an address that is not the one that was written to the pc register are discarded, together with the sub-tree of instruction instances below them.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: Transitions specific to load instructions:
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: [circle] * : At this point the memory footprint of the load instruction is provisionally known (it could change if earlier instructions are restarted) and its individual memory load operations can start being satisfied.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: An instance of a non-AMO load instruction, after being fetched, will typically experience the following transitions in this order:
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: . . . and/or (as many as needed to satisfy all the load operations of the instance) . . .
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: Before, between, and after the transitions above, any number of transitions may appear.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: In addition, a transition for fetching the instruction in the next program location will be available until it is taken.

sentences.text: This concludes the informal description of the operational model.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: The following sections describe the formal operational model.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

sentences.text: The intra-instruction semantics for each instruction instance is expressed as a state machine, essentially running the instruction pseudocode.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Intra-instruction Pseudocode Execution

sentences.text: Given a pseudocode execution state, it computes the next state.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Intra-instruction Pseudocode Execution

sentences.text: Most states identify a pending memory or register operation, requested by the pseudocode, which the memory model has to do.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Intra-instruction Pseudocode Execution

sentences.text: The states are (this is a tagged union; tags in small-caps):

sentences.text: memvalue and regvalue are lists of bytes; * address is an integer of XLEN bits;

sentences.text: for load/store, kind identifies whether it is `lr/sc`, acquire-RCpc/release-RCpc, acquire-RCsc/release-RCsc, acquire-release-RCsc; * for fence, kind identifies whether it is a normal or TSO, and (for normal fences) the predecessor and successor ordering bits; * regname identifies a register and a slice thereof (start and end bit indices); and the continuations describe how the instruction instance will continue for each value that might be provided by the surrounding memory model (the loadcontinuation and readcontinuation take the value loaded from memory and read from the previous register write, the storecontinuation takes false for an `sc` that failed and true in all other cases, and rescontinuation takes false if the `sc` fails and true otherwise).
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Intra-instruction Pseudocode Execution

sentences.text: Notice that writing to memory is split into two steps, Storeea and Storememv: the first one makes the memory footprint of the store provisionally known, and the second one adds the value to be stored.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Intra-instruction Pseudocode Execution

sentences.text: We ensure these are paired in the pseudocode (Storeea followed by Storememv), but there may be other steps between them.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Intra-instruction Pseudocode Execution

sentences.text: The pseudocode of each instruction performs at most one store or one load, except for AMOs that perform exactly one load and one store.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Intra-instruction Pseudocode Execution

sentences.text: Those memory accesses are then split apart into the architecturally atomic units by the hart semantics (see and below).
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Intra-instruction Pseudocode Execution

sentences.text: Informally, each bit of a register read should be satisfied from a register write by the most recent (in program order) instruction instance that can write that bit (or from the hart’s initial register state if there is no such write).
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Intra-instruction Pseudocode Execution

sentences.text: Hence, it is essential to know the register write footprint of each instruction instance, which we calculate when the instruction instance is created (see the action of below).
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Intra-instruction Pseudocode Execution

sentences.text: We ensure in the pseudocode that each instruction does at most one register write to each register bit, and also that it does not try to read a register value it just wrote.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Intra-instruction Pseudocode Execution

sentences.text: Data-flow dependencies (address and data) in the model emerge from the fact that each register read has to wait for the appropriate register write to be executed (as described above).
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Intra-instruction Pseudocode Execution

sentences.text: Each instruction instance i has a state comprising:
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Instruction Instance State

sentences.text: programloc, the memory address from which the instruction was fetched; * instructionkind, identifying whether this is a load, store, AMO, fence, branch/jump or a `simple` instruction (this also includes a kind similar to the one described for the pseudocode execution states); * srcregs, the set of source regnames (including system registers), as statically determined from the pseudocode of the instruction; * dstregs, the destination regnames (including system registers), as statically determined from the pseudocode of the instruction; * pseudocodestate (or sometimes just `state` for short), one of (this is a tagged union; tags in small-caps): +
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Instruction Instance State

sentences.text: [cols="<,<",grid="none"] | |Plain(isastate) |- ready to make a pseudocode transition
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Instruction Instance State

sentences.text: |Pendingmemloads(loadcontinuation) |- requesting memory load operation(s)
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Instruction Instance State

sentences.text: |Pendingmemstores(storecontinuation) |- requesting memory store operation(s) | * regreads, the register reads the instance has performed, including, for each one, the register write slices it read from; * regwrites, the register writes the instance has performed; * memloads, a set of memory load operations, and for each one the as-yet-unsatisfied slices (the byte indices that have not been satisfied yet), and, for the satisfied slices, the store slices (each consisting of a memory store operation and subset of its byte indices) that satisfied it. * memstores, a set of memory store operations, and for each one a flag that indicates whether it has been propagated (passed to the shared memory) or not. * information recording whether the instance is committed, finished, etc.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Instruction Instance State

sentences.text: Each memory load operation includes a memory footprint (address and size).
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Instruction Instance State

sentences.text: Each memory store operations includes a memory footprint, and, when available, a value.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Instruction Instance State

sentences.text: A load instruction instance with a non-empty memloads, for which all the load operations are satisfied (i.e. there are no unsatisfied load slices) is said to be entirely satisfied.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Instruction Instance State

sentences.text: Informally, an instruction instance is said to have fully determined data if the load (and `sc`) instructions feeding its source registers are finished.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Instruction Instance State

sentences.text: Similarly, it is said to have a fully determined memory footprint if the load (and `sc`) instructions feeding its memory operation address register are finished.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Instruction Instance State

sentences.text: Formally, we first define the notion of fully determined register write: a register write w from regwrites of instruction instance i is said to be fully determined if one of the following conditions hold:
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Instruction Instance State

sentences.text: . i is finished; or . the value written by w is not affected by a memory operation that i has made (i.e. a value loaded from memory or the result of `sc`), and, for every register read that i has made, that affects w, the register write from which i read is fully determined (or i read from the initial register state).
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Instruction Instance State

sentences.text: Now, an instruction instance i is said to have fully determined data if for every register read r from regreads, the register writes that r reads from are fully determined.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Instruction Instance State

sentences.text: An instruction instance i is said to have a fully determined memory footprint if for every register read r from regreads that feeds into i’s memory operation address, the register writes that r reads from are fully determined.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Instruction Instance State

sentences.text: The model state of a single hart comprises:
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Hart State

sentences.text: hartid, a unique identifier of the hart; * initialregisterstate, the initial register value for each register; * initialfetchaddress, the initial instruction fetch address; * instructiontree, a tree of the instruction instances that have been fetched (and not discarded), in program order.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Hart State

sentences.text: The model state of the shared memory comprises a list of memory store operations, in the order they propagated to the shared memory.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Shared Memory State

sentences.text: When a store operation is propagated to the shared memory it is simply added to the end of the list.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Shared Memory State

sentences.text: When a load operation is satisfied from memory, for each byte of the load operation, the most recent corresponding store slice is returned.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Shared Memory State

sentences.text: Each of the paragraphs below describes a single kind of system transition.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions

sentences.text: The description starts with a condition over the current system state.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions

sentences.text: The transition can be taken in the current state only if the condition is satisfied.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions

sentences.text: The condition is followed by an action that is applied to that state when the transition is taken, in order to generate the new system state.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions

sentences.text: A possible program-order-successor of instruction instance i can be fetched from address loc if:
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Fetch instruction

sentences.text: . it has not already been fetched, i.e., none of the immediate successors of i in the hart’s instructiontree are from loc; and . if i’s pseudocode has already written an address to pc, then loc must be that address, otherwise loc is: * for a conditional branch, the successor address or the branch target address; * for a (direct) jump and link instruction (`jal`), the target address; * for an indirect jump instruction (`jalr`), any address; and * for any other instruction, i.programloc+4.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Fetch instruction

sentences.text: Action: construct a freshly initialized instruction instance i' for the instruction in the program memory at loc, with state Plain(isastate), computed from the instruction pseudocode, including the static information available from the pseudocode such as its instructionkind, srcregs, and dstregs, and add i' to the hart’s instructiontree as a successor of i.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Fetch instruction

sentences.text: The possible next fetch addresses (loc) are available immediately after fetching i and the model does not need to wait for the pseudocode to write to pc; this allows out-of-order execution, and speculation past conditional branches and jumps.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Fetch instruction

sentences.text: For most instructions these addresses are easily obtained from the instruction pseudocode.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Fetch instruction

sentences.text: The only exception to that is the indirect jump instruction (`jalr`), where the address depends on the value held in a register.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Fetch instruction

sentences.text: In principle the mathematical model should allow speculation to arbitrary addresses here.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Fetch instruction

sentences.text: The exhaustive search in the `rmem` tool handles this by running the exhaustive search multiple times with a growing set of possible next fetch addresses for each indirect jump.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Fetch instruction

sentences.text: The initial search uses empty sets, hence there is no fetch after indirect jump instruction until the pseudocode of the instruction writes to pc, and then we use that value for fetching the next instruction.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Fetch instruction

sentences.text: Before starting the next iteration of exhaustive search, we collect for each indirect jump (grouped by code location) the set of values it wrote to pc in all the executions in the previous search iteration, and use that as possible next fetch addresses of the instruction.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Fetch instruction

sentences.text: This process terminates when no new fetch addresses are detected.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Fetch instruction

sentences.text: An instruction instance i in state Plain(Loadmem(kind, address, size, loadcontinuation)) can always initiate the corresponding memory load operations.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Initiate memory load operations

sentences.text: Construct the appropriate memory load operations mlos: * if address is aligned to size then mlos is a single memory load operation of size bytes from address; * otherwise, mlos is a set of size memory load operations, each of one byte, from the addresses address...address+size−1. . set memloads of i to mlos; and . update the state of i to Pendingmemloads(loadcontinuation).
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Initiate memory load operations

sentences.text: For a non-AMO load instruction instance i in state Pendingmemloads(loadcontinuation), and a memory load operation mlo in i.memloads that has unsatisfied slices, the memory load operation can be partially or entirely satisfied by forwarding from unpropagated memory store operations by store instruction instances that are program-order-before i if:
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Satisfy memory load operation by forwarding from unpropagated stores

sentences.text: . all program-order-previous `fence` instructions with `.sr` and `.pw` set are finished; . for every program-order-previous `fence` instruction, f, with `.sr` and `.pr` set, and `.pw` not set, if f is not finished then all load instructions that are program-order-before f are entirely satisfied; . for every program-order-previous `fence.tso` instruction, f, that is not finished, all load instructions that are program-order-before f are entirely satisfied; . if i is a load-acquire-RCsc, all program-order-previous store-releases-RCsc are finished; . if i is a load-acquire-release, all program-order-previous instructions are finished; . all non-finished program-order-previous load-acquire instructions are entirely satisfied; and . all program-order-previous store-acquire-release instructions are finished;
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Satisfy memory load operation by forwarding from unpropagated stores

sentences.text: Let msoss be the set of all unpropagated memory store operation slices from non-`sc` store instruction instances that are program-order-before i and have already calculated the value to be stored, that overlap with the unsatisfied slices of mlo, and which are not superseded by intervening store operations or store operations that are read from by an intervening load.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Satisfy memory load operation by forwarding from unpropagated stores

sentences.text: The last condition requires, for each memory store operation slice msos in msoss from instruction i':
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Satisfy memory load operation by forwarding from unpropagated stores

sentences.text: that there is no store instruction program-order-between i and i' with a memory store operation overlapping msos; and * that there is no load instruction program-order-between i and i' that was satisfied from an overlapping memory store operation slice from a different hart.
sentences.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Satisfy memory load operation by forwarding from unpropagated stores

sentences.text: . update i.memloads to indicate that mlo was satisfied by msoss; and . restart any speculative instructions which have violated coherence as a result of this, i.e., for every non-finished instruction i' that is a program-order-successor of i, and every memory load operation mlo' of i' that was satisfied from msoss', if there exists a memory store operation slice msos' in msoss', and an overlapping memory store operation slice from a different memory store operation in msoss, and msos' is not from an instruction that is a program-order-successor of i, restart i' and its restart-dependents.

sentences.text: This will involve, mostly, writing Sail code for the instructions, with minimal, if any, changes to the concurrency model. * The model covers only normal memory accesses (it does not handle I/O accesses). * The model does not cover TLB-related effects. * The model assumes the instruction memory is fixed.

sentences.text: In particular, the transition does not generate memory load operations, and the shared memory is not involved in the transition.

sentences.text: Instead, the model depends on an external oracle that provides an opcode when given a memory location. * The model does not cover exceptions, traps and interrupts.
